fssue A:
Do PERS Board Members, officers or emplovees manaqe ttpublic

pubic investrnents

rrwe construe the term tpublic i,nvestrnents t to

or bonds, and evidences of indebtedness received or

(penal
code Section 426. ) Therefore, the scope of the

The monies which are held and invested by PERS are not public
moneys or part of the general fund, but rather are trust funds
pursuant to both the california Constitution and the Government
code .
California Constitution article xvI, section 17(a) states as
follows:
tr (a) The assets of a public pension or
retirement systen are trust funds and sha1l be held
for the exclusive purposes of providing benefits to
participants in the pension or retirement system and
their beneficiaries and defraying reasonable expenses
of adroinistering the system. tt
Government Code section 2o2oo provides as follows in pertinent
part:
rr. . . The Pub1ic Employeest Retirement Fund is
a trust fund created, and adrninistered in accordance
with the provisions of this part, soleIy for the
benefit of the members and retired members of the
System and their survivors and beneficiaries. tr
PERS is funded by (1) employer contributions and (2) enployee
contributions, and (3) the interest thereon. PERS does not
borrow money fron the State and is not indebted to the State.
Hence, it would not appear that any PERS officials or empJ,oyees
including, but not lirnited to, PERS Board Members, the Chief
Executive officer, Assistant Executi.ve staff, the Investment
Manager or any investment staff rnember would fall within the
definition of a I'public official 
r^rho nanageIs] pub]ic
investments 
rr Therefore, it would appear that no PERS
Board Menber, officer or enployee should be subject to the
broad reporting requirements of Government Code section 872OO.
If you disagree r{rith PERS I conclusion in this regard, please
provide us nith an explanation which supports your conclusions.

Are individuals who hold the positions discussed be1ow. rrpublic
officials

872OO, PERS requests the Fppcrs opinion as to whether

and job duty statements, which are attached as Appendix B, are


scott HaIIibriD
August 2, 1.990
subject to the- broad repoting requj.renents of covernrnent Code
sectioh 872oo. r
We have attached the nost current PERS organizational chart to
assist in your deterninations in this regard. (Appendix c.)
By way of background, covernnent Code section 2o2o1 gives the
PERS Board lexclusive control of the administration and
investnent of the RetireDent Fund.r. Government Code section
20205.6 states thatrrltlhe board has exclusive control of the
i.nvestDent of the retirenent fund."
The Chief Executive Officer of PERS I'nonitors the vork of
j.nvestment staffrr and "directs . , . investnents.rr (See
attached duty statement in Appendix B,) The Chief Investment
Officer (a1so known as the Investnent Manager) is a contract
position. This position reports directly to the Chief
Executive officer. 
The Assistant Executive officer $ho i-s in
charge of investnent operations reports to the Chief Executive
Officer. While there are currently four Assistant Executive
Officers, it is PERS' position that gEIy the Assistant
Executive officer over investment operations makes investnent
decisions. Other internal ihvestnent staff report to their
various supervisors as set forth on their duty statements.
(See Appendix B.) It is PERS' position that these internal
staff nenbers should renain designated enptoyees on PERS
Conftict of Interest Code, because their work is subject to
several levels of revie!, and supervision.
Neither the ceneral Counsel nor the Assistant ceneral Counsel
nake decisions to buy or seLl investments. Under the direction
of the Board, individuals holding these positions prepare and
present shareholder proposals to corporations in vhich PERS is
a shareholder, oversee the lrork of outside investmen!
consultants, and represent and/or oversee representation of
PERS in legal proceedings involvj.ng coDpanies in irhich PERS
invests funds,
1 These positions are
agency Conflict of Interest all presently designated in PERS'
code,
3


Scott Hallibrin
August 2, 1990
Issue C:
Do Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 applv to
individuals (other than mernbers of state boards and
commissionsl who are subiect to the broad reporting
recruirements of covernment Code section 87200?
Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 expressly apply
to members of state boards or comnissions and trdesionated
c4p.IgyecE. 'l
Pursuant to covernment Code section 82019(d), the terrn
rrdesj.gnated employeetr does not include rr. . . any public
official 
specified in Section a72oo. . . .rr
Thus, to the extent that the FPPC determines that certain PERS
officers or employees are subject to the broad reporting
requirements of covernment Code section 872OO, it is PERSI
position that these officers and employees are not designated
employees, and hence, not subject to the prohibitions and
linits of Government Code section 89503, 89505 or 89506.
Further, it is PERS I position that the FPPC is without
authority to promulgate regulations which would include
individuals specified in Government Code section 872OO, within
the perview of sections 89503-89506.
PERS is aware of Governnent Code section 83LL2 which authorizes
the Fair Political Practices Cornmission to |tadopt, anend and
rescind rules and regulations to carry out the purposes and
provisions of this tit1e, . . .rr However, PERS is also a$/are
that this statute further expressly states that:
rrThese rules and regulations [adopted by the
FPPCI . . sha1l be consistent with this title 
and
other applicable law.rl
PERS further notes the well-settled lega1 principle that courts
not only rnay, but have an obligation to, strike down
administrative regulations which alter, amend, enLarge, or
inpair the scope of a statute. 
(Handlerv v. Franchise Tax
Board (1972) 26 Ca1 .App.3d 970, 981,.)
4


scott Hallibrin
August 2, l99O
Issue D:
How does the passa('e of Governnent Code sections 89506 and
La728?
Comraission Regulation 18728 states as follows in part:
tr(a) As used in this section, rhonorariumt means
a payment for speaking at any event, participating in
a panel or seroinar or engaging in any similar
activity. 
For purposes of this section, free
adrnission, food, beveraqes and sirnilar nominal
benefits provided to a filer at an event at which he
or she speaks, participates in a panel or seminar or
perfonns a similar service, and reimbursement or
advance for actual intrastate travel and for
necessarv accommodations provided directly in
connection with the event are not payments and need
not be reported bv the filer 
(Emphasis
added. )
Several PERS Board Members who are subject to covernnent Code
sections 89506 and 89504 are attenpting to plan trips in L991,.
Thus, PERS needs a response to this issue at your earliest
opportunity .
fssue E:
What chanqes to PERS Conflict of fnterest Code are required bv
Government Code sections 89503(c) and 89595(c)?
covernnent Code section 89503 inposes restrictions and
prohibitions on the receipt of honorariurn. Section 89505
imposes restrictions and prohibitions on the receipt of gifts.
Both sections require every state agency to amend its conflict
of Interest Code section no later than December 31, l-991 to
comply with these new provisions of 1aw.
In regard to the required amendments to the Conflict of
Interest Codes of state agencies, does the FppC have any
specific language which it suggests using? If not, wou-ld the
following lang"uage be acceptable:
5


scott Hallibrin
Augr.rst 2, L99O
IIPROHIBITIONS AND RESTRICTIONS APPLICABLE
TO AI.,I, DISCLOSURE CATEGORIES
rrNo Board rnerDber or designated ernployee shaI1 accept:
n1. An honorariun from any source, if the nember or empLoyee
would be required to report the receipt of incone or gifts
fron that source on his or her Stateuent of Economic
Interests. 
An honorariun is any payment in consideration
for any speech given, article published, or attendance at
any public or private conference, convention, meeting,
meaL or like gathering.
tt2. Gifts with a total value of more than two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) in a calendar year from any single source,
if the member or employee would be required to report the
receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
Statenent of Economic fnterests. rl
This language would be placed on paqe 4 of the Appendix to
PERS I Code, following the disclosure categories. 
(PERS'
current Code, is attached for your convenience as Appendix D.)
If our proposed language does not satisfy the reguirements of
Government Code sections 89503 (c) and 89505(c), please provide
us with sone alternative Language which PERS may use.
fssue F:
when does the 12 nonth period set forth in covernnent Code
section 87103(e) beoin te zun for discrual ification purposes?
Governrnent Code section 87103 states as follows:
'rAn official 
has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
material effect, distinguishable fron its effect on
the public generally, on the official 
or a rnenber of
his or her innediate fanily or on:
rr(e) any donor of, or any internediary or agent
for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or nore in value
provided to, received by, or premised to the public
official within 12 nonths prior to the tirne when the
deeision is made.
rr (Ernphasis added. )
6


Scott Halli.briu
August 2, 199 0
Doe6 one look to the date the decision 1s to be made and then
count back for 12 months? For exahple, AssuDe that Conpany X
gives a publie official a gift worth $50 on SepteDber L, L989i
another gift irorth $150 on JuIy 1, 1990r another gj.ft lrorth
Sl-00 on August 1, 1990, and another gift r,rorth 9100 on
Decenber 31, 1990. Could the public official in this exanple
have participated in a decision vhich affected Conpany X, on
Decenber l, L99O, because he had not received gifts aggregating
$250 in the 12 nonths prior to Decenber L, L99O?
Issue G:
Reportind Business Positions.
Are individuals who are subject to the broad reporting
requirenents of Governnent Code sections 87200 and 87203
required to report business positions r,rhich foreseeably nay be
affected naterially by any decision hade or participated in by
the designated employee by virtue of his or her position?
If so, l,hat is the legal authority for this reporting
requirenent and on which forn would this interest be reported?
If not, vhat is the ralionaLe for requirinq designated
employees to report such positions, but not requiring
individuals lrho repor! under Governuen! Code sectj.on 87200 to
report such positions?
Issue H:
Disqualif ications Issues.
Are the statutes and regulations rrhich govern disqual ification,
the saue for public officials who are subject to the broad
reporting requirenents of Governnent Code section 87200, as
they are for designated eDpLoyees under PERS' Conftict of
Interest Code? If not, please provide us vith an explanation
of hov and lrhen individuals subject to the broad discLosure
requireDents Dust disqualify thenselves, and of how and when
designed euployees under PERSr Code nust disqualify thenselves.
7
Thank
1egal"

sections.question: Issue A: Do PERS Board Members, officers or employees manage "public investments" within the meaning of the term as used in Government code section 87200? Issue B: Are individuals who hold the positions discussed below, "public officials who manage public investments" within the meaning of Government Code section 87200? Issue C: Do Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 apply to individuals (other than members of state boards and commissions) who are subject to the broad reporting requirements of Government Code section 87200? Issue D: How does the passage of Government Code sections 89506 and La728? Issue E: What changes to PERS Conflict of Interest Code are required by Government Code sections 89503(c) and 89595(c)? Issue F: When does the 12 month period set forth in Government Code section 87103(e) begin to run for disqualification purposes? Issue G: Reporting Business Positions.
sections.question_synthetic: PERS requests FPPC written advice on seven issues regarding whether PERS board members, officers, and employees are subject to Government Code sections 87200, 89503-89506 and related provisions, how to amend the PERS Conflict of Interest Code, and clarification on the 12-month lookback period for gift aggregation in disqualification analysis.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from PERS to FPPC, not an FPPC response. Contains seven distinct issues (A-G) with detailed factual background and legal arguments. Document appears truncated at Issue G. No FPPC conclusion/advice sections present. High confidence in identification as incoming request; moderate confidence due to truncation and complex multi-issue format.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Issue A: Do PERS Board Members, officers or employees manage "public investments" within the meaning of the term as used in Government code section 87200? Issue B: Are individuals who hold the positions discussed below, "public officials who manage public investments" within the meaning of Government Code section 87200? Issue C: Do Government Code sections 89503, 89505 and 89506 apply to individuals (other than members of state boards and commissions) who are subject to the broad reporting requirements of Government Code section 87200? Issue D: How does the passage of Government Code sections 89506 and La728? Issue E: What changes to PERS Conflict of Interest Code are required by Government Code sections 89503(c) and 89595(c)? Issue F: When does the 12 month period set forth in Government Code section 87103(e) begin to run for disqualification purposes? Issue G: Reporting Business Positions.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from California Public Employees' Retirement System (PERS) seeking FPPC written advice on multiple conflict of interest law questions, including whether PERS officials manage "public investments" under Government Code section 87200, which PERS positions are subject to reporting requirements, required amendments to the PERS Conflict of Interest Code, and clarification on gift aggregation timing rules.
